title: Effect of isotropic errors on the complexity of Grover's algorithm
abstract: Isotropic errors have been shown to be immune to conventional error correction techniques. While general theoretical frameworks have been proposed to model such errors, there have been no studies so far analysing their concrete impact on practical use-cases. Here we explore the effect of isotropic errors on the complexity of Grover's search algorithm through numerical simulations, with an analysis of the impact on the algorithm's performance and success probability. The results provide insights into the robustness of Grover's algorithm against isotropic errors, highlighting potential challenges for implementations on noisy quantum hardware. All results presented here are obtained through numerical simulations using the open-source python library \texttt{isotropic} developed as part of this work. The source code, numerical simulations and documentation for the library are available online at https://www.github.com/lazyoracle/isotropic-error-analysis .
summary: This work investigates how isotropic quantum errors affect the performance of Grover's search algorithm. By simulating the noise, the authors demonstrate that these errors are difficult to correct and cause the algorithm's success probability to drop significantly. The findings suggest that the required computational overhead grows rapidly, potentially negating the algorithm's inherent quadratic speedup.
main_problem: The paper analyzes the concrete impact of isotropic errors on the complexity and performance of Grover's search algorithm.
main_result: Isotropic errors are immune to conventional error correction, and even small errors significantly degrade Grover's success probability, leading to an apparent exponential growth in required computational repetitions.
method: The study uses numerical simulations to model the application of isotropic noise to quantum states and analyzes the resulting degradation of the algorithm's success probability.
model_or_system: The model is quantum computation, specifically applying isotropic noise to the state evolution of Grover's search algorithm on an n-qubit system.
key_observables: Success probability ($p_e(n)$), required additional repetitions ($k(n)$), and the error variance ($\sigma$).
important_parameters: Isotropic error variance ($\sigma$), number of qubits ($n$), and the gate depth of the circuit.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes isotropic errors are uniform across all gates and focuses only on the single-marked-item case of Grover's algorithm.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the statevector spreading due to isotropic error (Hinton diagrams), the decay of success probability vs. iterations, and the exponential growth of required repetitions ($k$) versus problem size for various error strengths ($\sigma$).
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the theoretical immunity of isotropic errors to standard QEC. It then details the mathematical framework for generating the perturbed state and applies this framework to simulate Grover's algorithm, culminating in an analysis of the scaling of required computational overhead.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, quantitative study of a specific, difficult-to-correct noise model (isotropic errors) on a fundamental quantum algorithm, which is highly relevant for assessing the feasibility of near-term quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Value of the Density function f(σ, θ0) (left) and Distribution function F(σ, θ0) (right) for d = 2 for different values of σ.

Fig. 2: Hinton diagrams of the final statevector before (left) and after (right) applying an isotropic error with σ = 0.999 for a 3-qubit Grover circuit. We see that while the marked state |011⟩is still visible after adding the error, the population is now spread across many other states, which reduces the overall success probability.

Fig. 3: Success probability versus number of Grover iterations for different system sizes and isotropic error magnitudes. Each curve on each subplot represents a different value of σ, showing how the success probability evolves with the number of iterations for a given error strength.
Fig. 4: Additional number of repetitions required to achieve the error-free success probability as a function of problem size for different isotropic error magnitudes.
Fig. 5: Growth rate b as a function of isotropic error magnitude σ overlaid with predicted curve from theoretical mixture model based on total gate count.
